items.correct: Removing material from a solid block to reveal the form (e.g., carving stone).
items.why: Students confuse 'subtractive' with arithmetic. In sculpture, additive = building up (clay, welding); subtractive = taking away (carving). Michelangelo described 'freeing' figures from the marble - quintessential subtractive thinking.

items.correct: Colours opposite each other on the colour wheel that create strong contrast.
items.misconception: Colours that look similar and harmonise gently.
items.random_wrong: Colours used to flatter the artist.
items.why: Students confuse 'complementary' with 'matching nicely'. In colour theory, complementary colours (red/green, blue/orange, yellow/purple) intensify each other through CONTRAST, not similarity.

items.q: Why is preserving white in watercolour important?
items.correct: White comes from the paper showing through - there is no true white pigment in traditional watercolour.
items.misconception: You can simply paint white over other colours later.
items.random_wrong: Because white is the most expensive colour.
items.why: Students plan to add white at the end as in opaque media. Watercolour is transparent - highlights must be PRESERVED from the start by leaving paper untouched or using masking fluid.

items.q: Carving is which kind of process?
items.why: Correct answer: Subtractive. “Additive” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: Copper and tin. “Iron and zinc” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In which year was Rodin's bronze of The Thinker first cast?
items.why: The real figure is 1902. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
